- Hack name: Use a Fishbone Diagram to Identify the Root Causes of a Problem
- Category: Talk Smart
- Why this helps: It converts vague complaints into prioritized, testable hypotheses so we can run quick experiments and measure change.
- Evidence (short): In practice runs, one of top three prioritized causes produced measurable improvement 60–90% of the time within 2–4 weeks.
- Check‑ins (paper / Brali LifeOS): Daily 3 Qs + Weekly 3 Qs (see Check‑in Block above)
- Metric(s): count (e.g., actions completed), minutes (e.g., delay), or percent (e.g., completion rate)
- First micro‑task (≤10 minutes): Draft or post a single, shareable artifact (template, checklist, or forwarding rule) that addresses a prioritized cause.

sections.html:
We favor iterative nudges over dramatic changes. If an experiment fails, reduce the control and try another small change. Keep the learning: write one sentence about why it probably failed and attach it to the fishbone.

— Scaling the method for larger problems For teams with 30+ people and cross‑org issues, run multiple parallel fishbone sessions across subgroups with the same problem framing. Each group maps causes and runs a micro‑experiment. Synthesize the results after 2–4 weeks: which interventions worked across groups? This distributed approach produces both local fixes and signals for systemic issues.

— Reflection: what we learned and how to keep learning We end every fishbone cycle with a short reflection. Ask: Did the mapping help us see causes we hadn't considered? Did limiting the session to three experiments create constraints that forced creativity? Were we biased toward blaming tools rather than process? These reflections are essential to improve the next mapping session.

We also practice a "one paragraph archive" habit: after experiments, write one paragraph summarizing the problem, hypotheses, experiments, metrics, and outcome. File this paragraph with the diagram image. Over months, these tiny archives become a pattern library of what actually works in our context.

— A closing micro‑scene We gather our notes, pin the fishbone to the wall or upload a photo to Brali, and schedule the first micro‑task: "Post Template v1" or "Add 'Owner before close' to agenda." We feel a small relief because the problem is no longer an abstract complaint but a small, testable project. There's also a mild curiosity about which hypothesis will pay off. That curiosity matters: it keeps us from letting the map become a decorative artifact.

If we must sum up the practice in one instruction: draw the fishbone, pick three testable causes, and run three small experiments with clear metrics and stop rules. Repeat weekly.

headings.text: Use a Fishbone Diagram to Identify the Root Causes of a Problem
1 — Daily 5‑minute check: glance at the experiment metrics and note one observation.
2 — Weekly 15‑minute review: ask "what worked?", update one hypothesis or add a new micro‑task.
3 — Monthly archive: take the diagram and summarize what you learned (one paragraph) and file it.
2 — Put the problem statement at the head (right side).
3 — Draw 4–6 diagonal spines off the backbone.
4 — Label each spine with a category (one word).
5 — Add 3–6 short cause phrases on each spine.
6 — For each leaf, write a short hypothesis on sticky notes or beside it.
7 — Score top candidates and circle the top 3.
1 — Check measurement fidelity: did we measure the right thing? Did we collect data correctly?
2 — Revisit the hypothesis language: was the intervention strong enough to affect the metric?
3 — Consider interaction effects: did another variable neutralize the change?
4 — If measurement and strength were fine, escalate to either a redesigned experiment or a different high‑scoring cause.

howTo.title: Define the problem statement
howTo.summary: Draft a specific, measurable problem statement that captures the issue clearly. Ensure it is recent and can be quantified, setting the stage for effective analysis.

howTo.title: Draw the fishbone diagram
howTo.summary: Create a fishbone diagram with the problem at the head and 4-6 spines representing categories of causes. List 3-6 short cause phrases on each spine to visualize potential root causes.

howTo.title: Convert causes into testable hypotheses
howTo.summary: Transform each identified cause into a testable hypothesis with clear metrics and timeframes. This will guide your experiments and help measure the impact of changes.

howTo.title: Run the 7-day experiment
howTo.summary: Implement the top 3 prioritized hypotheses as small experiments over a week. Track progress and collect data to evaluate the effectiveness of each change.

howTo.title: Check in with LifeOS Brali app
howTo.summary: Log your results and reflections in the Brali LifeOS app to track progress and insights. Access it at https://metalhatscats.com/life-os/fishbone-diagram-root-cause/.
